entry.title: A Missing Signal in PO Monitoring: Why “Next Delivery Status” Should Consider Order Acknowledgement
entry.content:
Transparency in procurement monitoring is essential—especially when it comes to identifying truly overdue Purchase Order (PO) items. SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud provides analytical tools for this purpose, one of them being the Fiori app Monitor Purchase Order Items.

However, a closer look at the app reveals a functional gap that can easily lead to misleading conclusions:
The “Next Delivery Status” currently considers only the Next Schedule Line Date, even when a Next Order Acknowledgement Date is maintained and lies in the future.

This blog post outlines why this behavior is problematic, what opportunity it presents, and how a small enhancement could significantly improve operational efficiency and clarity.

The Current Behavior

In the Monitor Purchase Order Items app, the field Next Delivery Status indicates whether a PO item is on track or overdue.

What is considered today:

✔️Next Schedule Line Date

What is not considered:

❌ Next Order Acknowledgement Date

This means that even if:

  • An order acknowledgement has been received
  • A future acknowledged delivery date is maintained

…the system may still mark the PO item as “Overdue”—solely because the original schedule line date is in the past.

A screenshot taken on 03.02.2025 clearly illustrates this situation: The item is flagged as overdue, despite having a valid and future order acknowledgement date.

 

Why This Is a Problem

From a business perspective, this behavior makes it impossible to reliably distinguish between two very different situations:

  1. Items that are genuinely overdue
    (no acknowledgement, no updated delivery commitment)

  2. Items that are under control
    (acknowledged by the supplier with a future delivery date)

By treating both scenarios the same, the app introduces unnecessary noise into PO monitoring.

The Opportunity

The opportunity lies in enhancing the logic behind Next Delivery Status:

When a Next Order Acknowledgement Date is maintained and lies in the future, the status should reflect this and no longer be shown as “Overdue”.

This would not replace the schedule line date—but rather complement it by acknowledging the most recent and supplier-confirmed commitment.

Expected Business Benefits

Introducing this enhancement would deliver immediate and tangible value:

1️⃣Better traceability of overdue items

Users could clearly identify which items actually require follow-up attention.

2️⃣ Greater efficiency

Purchasing teams would spend less time investigating “false overdue” items and more time acting where it truly matters.

3️⃣ Improved trust in monitoring apps

Statuses would better reflect real-world supplier communication instead of purely technical dates.

Why This Matters

The purpose of monitoring applications is not just to display data—but to support decision-making.

If acknowledged future delivery dates are ignored in status logic, the resulting indicators can unintentionally mislead users, trigger unnecessary escalations, or reduce confidence in analytical tools.

In an environment where supplier communication and flexibility are key, the system should reflect the most reliable and up-to-date information available.

Call to Action

If you have faced similar situations where PO items are flagged as overdue despite valid order acknowledgements and you believe that the Next Delivery Status should take the Next Order Acknowledgement Date into account...

👉  Please Vote to Idea # 340221 Updating of "Next Delivery Status" based on "Next Order Acknowledgement Date" on SAP Influence and help move this enhancement forward.

entry.title: Re: ABAP Odata
entry.content:
Hello @ankitpurohitap 


1. CSRF issue

First call GET with header:
x-csrf-token: fetch
Copy token from response
Use it in POST header:
x-csrf-token: <token>
Also pass cookies

2. Make API accessible

Activate service in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE
Use system URL:
https://<host>:<port>/sap/opu/odata/...

 


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: Issue Filtering Empty Fields in OData Analytical Mode...
entry.content:
Hello 

 


OData now treats empty (`''`) and `null` differently, so use an explicit combined condition,

ZOFFICE ne '' and ZOFFICE ne null

not (ZOFFICE eq '' or ZOFFICE eq null)

length(ZOFFICE) gt 0

This ensures both empty strings and null values are excluded.

 

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: How to control the labels of associated fields?
entry.content:
Hello 

 

You can’t control labels of deep association fields directly in UI.SelectionFields.

Define the label on the target entity field instead

annotate service.TargetEntity with {
material_customer_customerfield @Common.Label: '{i18n>customer}';
};

Then just reference it in SelectionFields:

UI.SelectionFields: [ field1/material/customer_customerfield ]

 

 

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: Unexpected Exam Result – Failed Immediately After Submission (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Certification)
entry.content:
Hi @karim27 Karim27,

I’ve actually experienced the same situation on Sunday, April 26, when taking the SAP Certified - Implementation Consultant - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Sourcing and Procurement exam. After submitting, my result also appeared almost instantly and showed Failed, which honestly surprised me. I followed all the instructions step by step during the system simulation tasks and was quite confident that my answers were correct. Because of that, the immediate result felt confusing and made me question whether there might have been a technical issue or something unexpected in the evaluation process.

I’m currently in a similar position-considering retaking the exam, but also concerned that I might approach it in the same way again.

Thanks for bringing this up-glad to know I’m not the only one experiencing this.

entry.title: Create Sales Orders - AI-Assisted Extraction
entry.content:
The Create Sales Orders – AI‑Assisted Extraction app (F8947), available with SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2602, enables you to transform unstructured purchase order documents—such as PDFs and scanned images—into sales orders using SAP Document AI.
By automating data extraction from incoming documents, the app significantly reduces manual data entry, minimizes errors, and accelerates sales order processing—allowing your teams to work faster and more efficiently.

What’s new compared to Create Sales Orders – Automatic Extraction

The AI‑Assisted Extraction app introduces several powerful enhancements:

  • Embedded Edition of SAP Document AI
    Uses the embedded edition instead of the basic edition for higher accuracy and richer capabilities.

  • Multi‑Language Support
    Extracts data from purchase orders in multiple languages.

  • Instant Learning
    Continuously improves extraction accuracy based on user confirmations and corrections.

  • Schema Prompts
    Supports prompts at the schema level to further enhance extraction accuracy.

Prerequisites

Before using this app, ensure that the following prerequisites are met:
  1. The integration of SAP Document AI is set up. For more information, see Integration with SAP Document AI.

  2. The Extraction of Purchase Order (SALES_ORDER_REQUEST_EXTRACTION) schema is activated in the Manage Document AI Schema Registration Subscriptions app (F8996). For more information, see PURCHASE_ORDER_STANDARD and Manage Document AI Schema Registration Subscriptions

  3. Active the feature in SAP Discovery Center Business AI Feature by searching  "Sales Order Creation from Unstructured Data", refer to the note.

 

Authorizations

To access this app, users need to have the following IAM app assigned:
  • Create Sales Orders - AI-Assisted Extraction (F8947_TRAN)

The instant learning requires another IAM app:

  • Review Extractions for Sales Order Creation - SAP Document AI(F9153_TRAN)

Usage

The basic Usage is the same as the Create Sales Orders - Automatic Extraction .

However, AI‑Assisted Extraction adds advanced learning and correction capabilities, described below.

Instant Learning

Instant Learning allows the system to improve extraction accuracy over time based on your feedback.
Prerequisites:

  1. Your user has been assigned with business catalog Sales - AI-Assisted Extraction for Sales Order Creation (SAP_SD_BC_SO_DOCUMENTAI_PC) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

  2. Your user has write access to purchase order documents in the SAP Document AI workspace. Sufficient authorization must have been assigned to your user via the Identity Authentication service on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP). For more information, see Subscribing to the SAP Document AI Workspace With the Identity Authentication Service. 

     

  3. Instant learning has been activated in SAP Document AI. For more information, see Instant Learning.

When the system makes a mistake extracting information, you correct it. By confirming that fix, you’re teaching the AI how to do it right the next time. Over time, it gets smarter and more accurate, specifically for the types of documents your business uses.

If you notice an extraction result isn’t perfect, you can easily correct it and train the model for the future.

Click the "Review Extractions" button under the "SAP Document AI" menu. This will take you to the SAP Document AI workspace.

 
Here, you can correct any data that was extracted incorrectly.
 
To feed this correction back into the AI model, simply click "Confirm" on the document. Don’t worry if the status temporarily changes to "In Progress"—it will soon update to "Confirmed," and your input will be used for instant learning.

Refresh Sales Order 

This function is incredibly useful after you’ve made corrections in the Document AI workspace.

  • Clicking "Refresh Sales Order" performs a complete update.
  • Important: This action will erase most existing data in the sales order request, including any manual changes you made directly in the app.
  • It then retrieves the latest extraction result—including any corrections you just saved or confirmed—and re-processes it to determine master data and apply relevant business logic (BAdIs).

 

By leveraging these new AI-powered features, you can significantly reduce manual effort and turn your sales order creation process into a smarter, faster, and more reliable operation.

Prompts on Schema

Prerequisites:
  1. The Admin or SchemaAdmin authorization has been assigned to your user in the SAP Document AI workspace (see Subscribing to the SAP Document AI Workspace With the Identity Authentication Service).

Log in to the SAP Document AI workspace and click Schema. For details on how to access the workspace, see the help document: https://help.sap.com/docs/document-ai/sap-document-ai/using-sap-document-ai-workspace?locale=en-US&version=LATEST

Locate the schema SALES_ORDER_REQUEST, version 1, which the app uses.

Within the schema, open the Processing Settings section.

Note: To edit the schema, click the Edit button in the section header—not the one in the page header—to avoid changing the schema version. The app is available only for version 1 of the SALES_ORDER_REQUEST schema.

For more information, see this help document: https://help.sap.com/docs/document-ai/sap-document-ai/processing-instructions?locale=en-US&version=LATEST

A Note on Pricing

It is important to be aware that the use of SAP Document AI is a metered service. This means it is based on usage and may incur additional costs. For specific details on licensing and pricing models, please refer to the official SAP documentation or consult with your SAP account executive.

For more information, see this link: https://help.sap.com/docs/document-ai/sap-document-ai/ai-features-for-embedded-edition

entry.title: Re: System is not blocking GR with 103 movt type
entry.content: There is no standard config change to make 103 behave like 101 for source inspection. It requires either a process change (use 101) or a technical enhancement (BAdI/user exit).

entry.title: 1H 2026 SuccessFactors Onboarding Enhancement: New Task Management Page for Onboarding Tasks
entry.content:
Introduction

Managing onboarding tasks at scale has always been messy. HR teams often juggle multiple systems, open dozens of employee records, and still struggle to answer simple but critical questions like:

  • Which tasks are overdue?
  • Are compliance documents complete?
  • Where are the bottlenecks?

With the 1H 2026 release, SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding introduces the Task Management Dashboard—a centralized, real-time view of all onboarding and compliance tasks across your organization.

No more digging. No more guesswork. Just clarity.

Why This Enhancement Matters

The new dashboard isn’t just a UI improvement—it fundamentally changes how HR teams manage onboarding:

  • Complete Visibility – View onboarding, compliance, and custom tasks in one place
  • Time Efficiency – Eliminate the need to open individual candidate records
  • Proactive Management – Spot overdue or risky tasks early
  • Compliance Confidence – Track critical activities like I-9 and e-signatures centrally

Key Use Cases

  1. Identifying Overdue Tasks Across All New Hires

Scenario: HR needs a quick overview of overdue tasks.

Steps:

  • Navigate to the Task Management Dashboard
  • Apply filter: Status = Overdue
  • Sort by Due Date
  • Review and assign follow-ups

 

Result:
A complete overview in under 2 minutes—saving 30+ minutes weekly compared to manual tracking.

 

  1. Tracking Compliance Task Completion

Scenario: Verify all I-9 and E-Verify tasks are complete

Steps:

  • Open the dashboard
  • Filter by Task Group = Form I-9 and E-Verify
  • Filter by status (Overdue / Nearing Deadline / On Track)
  • Review for audit readiness

Result:
Instant compliance reporting—saving 1–2 hours of manual effort.

Before vs. Now

Before:

  • Navigate through individual candidate records
  • Maintain external spreadsheets
  • Discover issues reactively
  • No unified compliance tracking

Now:

  • Centralized dashboard across all candidates
  • Real-time task status updates
  • Advanced filtering and sorting
  • Integrated onboarding + compliance tracking

Dashboard Features Explained

The Task Management Dashboard provides powerful filters and controls:

  • Employee Name – Search tasks for a specific individual
  • Task Status:
    • Overdue – Past due and incomplete
    • Nearing Deadline – Within 7 days
    • On Track – More than 7 days remaining
  • Task Group – Categories like:
    • New Hire Activity
    • Compliance Forms
    • Data Review
    • Custom Tasks
  • Task Type – Granular task-level filtering
  • Due Date Range – Filter tasks within a timeframe
  • Go Button – Apply filters instantly
  • Task List View – Displays results with total count

 

Configuration & Prerequisites

To enable the dashboard:

  1. Enable Feature
    • Admin Center → General Settings → Activate Task Management Dashboard
  2. Assign Permissions
    • Grant access to HR Managers, HRBPs, and Admins
  3. System Requirement
    • Must use Onboarding 2.0
  4. Role-Based Permissions (RBP)
    • Ensure correct RBP are provided with required target population

Limitations to Consider

  • Currently only Onboarding and compliance tasks associated with onboarding process are supported. Offboarding tasks are not supported
  • We can only see the HM/ HR tasks. The New hire tasks like PDC, I-9 section 1 cannot be monitored using the Task Management Page.
  • RBP Security – Users only see tasks they have permission to view based on role-based permissions.

Optional Enhancement: Quick Access

You can create a custom quick link to the Task Management Dashboard on the homepage.

Guide reference for configuration Managing Your Onboarding Tasks

Key Takeaways

  • Central Visibility: One unified dashboard for all tasks
  • Massive Time Savings: Reduce hours of tracking to minutes
  • Proactive HR Operations: Address issues before escalation
  • Available Now: Included in 1H 2026 release

Conclusion

The Task Management Dashboard brings a long-needed shift from reactive tracking to proactive onboarding management.

Instead of scattered data and manual effort, HR teams now get:

  • Clear visibility
  • Faster decision-making
  • Stronger compliance control

Getting Started

  1. Enable the dashboard in Admin Center
  2. Assign user permissions
  3. Train HR teams on filters and usage
  4. Add dashboard review to weekly routines

Final Thoughts

This enhancement in SAP’s ecosystem reflects a broader trend: simplifying HR operations through intelligent centralization.

Visit New Task Management Page for Onboarding Tasks for detailed documentation. Share how this dashboard transforms your onboarding workflow!

Disclaimer

This post and SAP's strategy and possible future developments are subject to change and may be changed by SAP at any time for any reason without notice. This is not a commitment, promise or legal obligation to deliver any material, code or functionality. This post is provided without warranty of any kind, either express or implied. SAP assumes no responsibility for errors or omissions except if caused by SAP's intentional or gross negligence. Forward-looking statements are subject to risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results to differ materially from expectations.

Applies to: SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding 1H 2026+

entry.title: Re: Pop up Address field issue in Sales order
entry.content: If your requirement is mandatory visibility of Floor, Building, Region, City during address selection, you’ll need a technical enhancement. The popup cannot be extended via simple customizing. The cleanest approach is a BAdI implementation that fetches and displays those extra ADRC fields.

entry.title: Re: Custom Batch Number Creation for Process Order in SAP...
entry.content:
Direct modification of COR1, COR2, COR6N, MIGO is not possible in Public Cloud. SAP does not allow custom logic to override batch number assignment in these transactions the way you could in on-premise (via exits like EXIT_SAPMM07M_001).

Having said that, the recommended approach would be

  • Set batch number range to external in configuration.
  • Use Custom Logic (released BAdIs in Public Cloud) to auto-derive and propose the batch number from your Z-table.
  • This way, the system does not generate internally, but your logic fills the batch field consistently across COR1, COR2, COR6N, and MIGO.
  • Full suppression of internal number range (like in on-premise exits) is not supported in Public Cloud.

entry.title: Re: Can we have person id set different than User ID via...
entry.content:
Hi @IMZEI ,

Person ID can’t be controlled via Business Rules or Integration Centre.

In SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central, person-id-external is system-managed — it’s either generated via number range (Next Person ID Assigned) or provided during import. There’s no rule scenario available to generate or override it like User ID.

Integration Centre also won’t help since it doesn’t handle ID creation during hire.

Ref: SAP KBA 2493579, EC Implementation Guide (Person ID vs User ID)

Hope this helps.

Regards,
Xaheer

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud invoice output print Preview
entry.content:
Dear @ramesh_babu472e30,

Recommended steps:

1. Open Manage Billing Documents app F0797

2. Search and select the billing document / invoice

3. Choose Display

 

4. Choose Preview

SAP also documents the same alternative path via Display Billing Documents app VF03:

1. Open Display Billing Documents

2. Enter the billing document number

3. Go to Goto ->Header ->Output

 

4. Select the output item

5. Choose Display Document

Important point

The preview is available only when the billing document has a valid output item. If the system shows No Preview Available, SAP explains this is usually because no available output item exists for the billing document. In that case, check Output Parameter Determination / output item creation first

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3396424

Best Regards,

Dawid

1. Warehouse No.2. Putaway Control3. Whse Process Type/Group4. Qty Classific.5. Stock Type/Group6. Type7. Usage8. Party Entitled to Dispose9. Hazard Rating 110. Hazard Rating 2

Even after doing all STSS and WPT configuration and mapping unable to solve this error what can I do?

entry.title: Re: ORDRSP/ORDERS05 IDoc Not Validating Plant (WERKS) in...
entry.content:
In standard SAP for ORDRSP / ORDERS05 (process code ORDR), the inbound IDoc processing does not validate the plant (WERKS) field in segment E1EDP01 against customizing or master data. The system assumes that the plant sent is correct and simply updates the schedule line confirmation. That is why even an invalid plant code is accepted without error.

If validation is mandatory, you must implement a custom enhancement (BAdI or custom FM) to enforce WERKS checks. In your inbound ORDRSP/ORDERS05 processing, add a check against TVKWZ using the sales area from the IDoc (sales org, distribution channel, division) and the plant (WERKS). If no valid assignment exists, raise an error (status 51) and stop the IDoc posting. This aligns with how the standard sales order creation logic validates plants, so your IDoc processing will behave consistently with online order entry. The other option is in WE57/WE42, you can assign a custom function module to the ORDR process code. This module can validate WERKS before calling the standard update logic.

entry.title: Re: MM Period Issue
entry.content: 1. Gestion des périodes Logistiques (MM) C'est le point le plus sensible pour la Supply Chain. Contrairement à la finance, SAP ne permet généralement d'avoir que deux périodes ouvertes en MM (la période actuelle et la précédente). Le défi : Si vous clôturez mars en mai, vous risquez de bloquer les mouvements de stocks. Action : Utilisez l'application "Mise à jour des périodes logistiques" (F0012 - Close Periods). Tant que vous ne lancez pas manuellement la clôture de la période de mars (pour passer à avril, puis mai), mars restera "ouvert" pour les mouvements de marchandises. Attention : Si vous restez sur mars, vous ne pourrez pas poster de mouvements sur mai tant que vous n'aurez pas avancé la période. 2. Gestion des périodes Comptables (FI) Pour que vos flux Supply Chain (réceptions, expéditions) génèrent des écritures comptables en mars, la période doit être ouverte en Finance. Application : "Gestion des périodes comptables" (F0001 - Manage Posting Periods). Configuration : Sélectionnez votre variante de période comptable. Pour le type de compte "+" (Tous) et "M" (Matériel), assurez-vous que l'intervalle de dates inclut mars (ex: 03.202X à 05.202X). Cela permet de saisir des documents avec une date de pièce en mars tout en étant physiquement en mai.

entry.title: Re: Closing individual WBS elements in S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Below is the Best Practices

  • Use TECO when you want to stop new commitments but still allow settlement.
  • Use Closed only after settlement is complete and no further postings are expected.
  • Apply status changes at the WBS element level to manage granular closure (e.g., closing completed work packages while keeping others open).
  • Document closure decisions in project governance to avoid accidental blocking of required postings.

entry.title: Integration Paths Between SAP PaPM Cloud and SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

This short blog post explores the integration paths between SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud (SAP PaPM Cloud) and SAP Business Data Cloud (SAP BDC).

SAP BDC is architected to directly integrate with a defined set of supported consumer applications. This design is outlined in the official documentation on Data Products in SAP Business Data Cloud. In this context, SAP PaPM Cloud complements the overall architecture by leveraging its flexibility to integrate through these SAP BDC–supported consumers.

As a prerequisite, Data Products needs to be installed and activated. If you would like to learn more about how Data Products are integrated with SAP and non‑SAP applications, including Data Product sharing, installation, and activation, please refer to the official SAP documentation listed below.

  • SAP Business Data Cloud – Overview
    Provides the foundational concepts of SAP Business Data Cloud, including Data Products and their role in SAP and third‑party integrations.
  • Activating Data Packages and Installing Data Products
    Describes how Data Products are activated in SAP BDC and installed into SAP Datasphere, including supported access methods.
  • Governing and Publishing Data in the Catalog
    Explains how Data Products can be shared from SAP BDC to supported SAP target systems such as SAP Datasphere, SAP HANA Cloud, and others.
  • Data Product Support in SAP HANA Cloud
    Details how SAP BDC Data Products can be installed, queried, and consumed within SAP HANA Cloud.

The table below summarizes the Supported and Feasibility proved in the SAP Documentation inbound and outbound integration paths, highlighting how data can flow into and out of SAP PaPM Cloud using standard SAP technologies and procedures.

Integration Direction

Integration Scenario with SAP BDC-Integrated Applications

Support Status & Context

Documentation / References

Inbound

SAP BDC Data Product → SAP Datasphere → SAP PaPM Cloud (via SDA)

✅Supported – Uses standard SAP Datasphere integration procedures

Integrating SAP PaPM Cloud & SAP Datasphere (in SAP BDC)

Inbound

SAP  BDC Data Product → SAP Datasphere → SAP PaPM Cloud (via SAP DSP’s Replication Flow)

✅Supported – Data Products activated from SAP BDC through SAP Datasphere into Local HANA Table in underlying SAP PaPM Cloud DB via Replication Flow

How DataSphere (in SAP BDC) push and pull data to SAP PaPM Cloud - UM via Replication Flow

Inbound

SAP Analytics Cloud Model → SAP PaPM Cloud (via Odata Connection)

✅Supported – Standard SAC-to-SAP PaPM Cloud integration procedures

•       SAC and SAP PaPM Cloud Integration in a Nutshell

•       How to setup Model View OData

Inbound

SAP BDC Data Product → SAP HANA Cloud via Delta Sharing (SAP PaPM underlying DB) → SAP PaPM Cloud (via HANA Connection)

✅ Supported – Feasibility proved in the SAP Documentation

Access and Query a Data Product in SAP HANA Cloud

Inbound

SAP BDC Data Product → SAP Databricks → SAP HANA Cloud via Delta Sharing (SAP PaPM underlying DB) → SAP PaPM Cloud (via HANA Connection)

✅ Supported – Feasibility proved in the SAP Documentation

Create a Databricks Delta Sharing Remote Source

Outbound

SAP PaPM Cloud Tables → SAP Datasphere Local Tables (via Replication Flow)

✅Supported – Standard replication flow procedures from SAP PaPM Cloud into SAP Datasphere local tables

How DataSphere (in SAP BDC) push and pull data to SAP PaPM Cloud - UM via Replication Flow

Outbound

SAP PaPM Cloud Calculation Views → SAP Analytics Cloud (via Live HANA Connection)

✅Supported – Standard SAP PaPM Cloud-to-SAC integration procedures

•       How SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) pulls data from SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud (SAP PaPM Cloud)

•       SAC and SAP PaPM Cloud Integration in a Nutshell

Outbound

SAP PaPM Cloud Y Tables / Artifacts → SAP Datasphere (via SDA)

✅Supported – Uses standard SAP Datasphere integration procedures

Integrating SAP PaPM Cloud & SAP Datasphere (in SAP BDC)

That’s it! I hope you learned how SAP PaPM Cloud can be strategically integrated with SAP BDC through supported SAP applications. With the right integration approach, organizations can maximize data value and drive meaningful business outcomes.

See you again in my next blog post!

entry.title: Why SAP Warehouse Logistics Moved Away from WKWebView for iOS Authentication
entry.content:
Why SAP Warehouse Logistics moved away from WKWebView for iOS authentication

If you manage SAP Warehouse Logistics in an enterprise environment, or if you're just curious about what goes on under the hood of mobile authentication on iOS, maybe this little look behind the scenes is of interest. We recently published SAP Note 3747394 about a change to the browser technology used for authentication in SAP Warehouse Logistics on iOS - and I want to explain not just what changed, but why it was necessary and what it means in practice.

Why authentication on iOS is more nuanced than it looks

Enterprise identity providers like Microsoft Entra ID support Conditional Access (CA) policies - rules that enforce requirements before granting access to an application. Common examples include:

  • Device compliance - is the device enrolled and meeting your organisation's security baseline?
  • Approved app controls - is the user authenticating through a Microsoft-approved application?
  • App protection policies - is Intune actively managing this app?

For Conditional Access to work correctly on iOS, the authentication flow must happen inside a browser context that the operating system recognises and can inspect. The key mechanism here is the Microsoft Enterprise SSO plug-in - a system-level extension provided by Microsoft Authenticator or the Intune Company Portal which intercepts authentication requests and enriches them with device compliance signals and app identity information. This only works if the authentication flow runs through the system browser stack. Not all iOS browser technologies qualify and that distinction matters more than most people realise.

From WKWebView to ASWebAuthenticationSession: what changed and why it matters

SAP Warehouse Logistics was previously using WKWebView for the OAuth onboarding flow. WKWebView is an embedded web view that runs entirely inside the app process. It is fast and customisable, but completely isolated from the system browser - no shared cookies, no single sign-on, and the Microsoft Enterprise SSO plug-in cannot intercept anything happening inside it. Microsoft explicitly does not support WKWebView for Entra ID sign-in under strict Conditional Access. When an organisation's policies enforce device compliance or app protection requirements, the authentication flow fails with error AADSTS53003.

If your users are already hitting AADSTS53003 when trying to onboard, this is exactly why - and the migration described here resolves it.

The solution is ASWebAuthenticationSession, Apple's dedicated API for OAuth and OIDC flows, available since iOS 12. Unlike WKWebView, it runs as a system-level authentication session - the same browser stack used by Safari. The Microsoft Enterprise SSO plug-in can intercept and enrich the flow, surfacing device compliance and app identity signals that Conditional Access policies evaluate. The app itself never has access to the session cookies; the OS handles the token exchange and returns only the result. (This is also why Apple his recommending this API for authentication flows - it is a deliberate privacy boundary, not just a technical convenience.)

Microsoft's own authentication library, MSAL, selects ASWebAuthenticationSession by default on iOS 12 and above. SAP Warehouse Logistics now does the same.

What triggered this change

A real customer deployment surfaced the incompatibility. Users in an organisation with strict Conditional Access policies - device compliance enforcement combined with app protection requirements - were unable to complete onboarding. The authentication flow failed with AADSTS53003, leaving users locked out with no actionable error in the app.

That incident made it clear that WKWebView would not hold up across the range of enterprise configurations customers are deploying. The migration to ASWebAuthenticationSession addresses the root cause directly, not just the symptom.

Why your existing QR codes will stop working

The migration to ASWebAuthenticationSession requires a server-side configuration update in SAP Mobile Services. The OAuth redirect URI changes from an https-based URL to a custom app URL scheme. Any QR codes generated before this update encode the old redirect URI and will no longer be valid after the change is applied.

This is a one-time change. The server-side update will be applied to all productive systems on May 4, 2026, between 9:00 and 10:00 CEST. Once your organisation is using the new QR code, future onboardings will work without any further intervention. (If a user has already successfully onboarded after May 4, no action is needed - the new configuration is already in place for their device.)

One thing to expect: a new system consent dialog

When users onboard with the new configuration, iOS will display a system-level consent dialog asking permission to sign in using the shared browser session. This dialog is shown by the operating system - it is not part of the app UI and cannot be customised. It appears once per authentication and is a normal part of how ASWebAuthenticationSession works. Users should simply confirm it to proceed.

What you need to do

Once the server-side update has been applied on May 4, 2026:

  1. Generate a new onboarding QR code from SAP Mobile Services.
  2. If you use an MDM solution such as Intune or Jamf, distribute the new AppConfig for the updated onboarding values to your users through your existing rollout process.
  3. Each user must uninstall SAP Warehouse Logistics, reinstall it, and complete onboarding using the new QR code.

This applies to both iOS and Android users - the server-side change affects all platforms equally.

Summary

The move from WKWebView to ASWebAuthenticationSession is a meaningful step for enterprise deployments. WKWebView's isolation from the system browser was the root cause of Conditional Access failures in tenants with strict Microsoft Entra ID policies - it simply cannot surface the device and app identity signals that Conditional Access needs to evaluate. ASWebAuthenticationSession runs in Apple's system browser stack, integrates with the Microsoft Enterprise SSO plug-in, and is the modern and recommended iOS browser technology that fully supports all Conditional Access controls. The QR code re-onboarding is a one-time effort, and the result is an authentication flow that works reliably across the full range of enterprise configurations.

Further reading

Full details and step-by-step instructions are available in SAP Note 3747394 on the SAP Support Portal.

entry.title: SAP Work Clearance Management (Permit to Work): Making Maintenance Safer and Compliant
entry.content:
SAP Work Clearance Management (Permit to Work):
Making Maintenance Safer and Compliant

 

Keeping people safe and plants running smoothly is a daily priority in high‑risk industries like chemicals, oil and gas, utilities, and manufacturing. SAP Work Clearance Management - Permit to Work (WCM) helps companies plan and execute maintenance work safely and in line with regulations.

Part of the broader SAP Environment, Health, and Safety (EHS) portfolio, SAP WCM sits alongside solutions that track emissions, manage regulatory duties, and monitor waste — all in support of corporate sustainability goals. See SAP’s EHS solution family here: SAP Environment, Health, and Safety Management product and portfolio.

What makes WCM practical is its tight integration with SAP Plant Maintenance. As an application component (PM‑WCM), it connects maintenance planning, asset management, and safety tasks in one flow. That means you can align work orders with the right permits, isolations, and approvals before anyone starts work.

With WCM, you can:

  • Define and control who is allowed to approve and perform high‑risk jobs.
  • Enforce safety steps such as lockout/tagout, fire and radiation protection, gas testing, and personal protective equipment (PPE).
  • Isolate technical objects to create safe working conditions — esp. for parallel jobs in the same area.
  • Track permits and safety measures centrally for audits and continuous improvement.

SAP offers packaged scope and accelerators that reflect these capabilities, such as the S/4HANA scope items for Work Clearance Management. These support scenarios from simple, automated permits to comprehensive isolation management.

By bringing permits, safety measures, and approvals into one integrated process, SAP WCM helps protect employees, improve system reliability, and demonstrate compliance — while supporting your sustainability strategy.

If you are new to the topic, short videos and demos provide a quick look at the user experience and value. These videos highlight how SAP Work Clearance Management (Permit to Work) supports these processes from a fully automated simple permit process up to a full-blown isolation management for parallel works in high-risk areas. Explore EHS product videos here:

SAP Work Clearance Management – Classic solution with SAP Gui

SAP Work Clearance Management – Permit to Work – overview

SAP Work Clearance Management – Permit to Work – Simple/automatic process

SAP Work Clearance Management – Permit to Work – Permits with simple safety measures

SAP Work Clearance Management – Permit to Work – Parallel work with complex approvals

SAP Work Clearance Management – Permit to Work – LoTo (Isolation Management)

For more on EHS and Operational Safety see also:
Occupational Health & Safety with SAP EHS, Work Clearance Management and Management of Change
Assuring Operational Excellence with SAP Work Clearance Management and Management of Change
Disclose Environmental Performance Data - Not only a question of regulations

For help and support:
Sustainability, EHS and Product Compliance Consulting Services | SAP

entry.title: Meet Me at SAPPHIRE — Let's Talk SAP Business Data Cloud 🚀
entry.content:
SAP SAPPHIRE is just around the corner, and I'm excited to share that I'll be presenting in Orlando this May! If you're heading to the event — or just curious about what's coming — here's a quick preview of what to expect.

My session: Introduction to SAP Business Data Cloud

SAP Business Data Cloud turned one year old — and what a year it's been. At SAPPHIRE 2026 in Orlando, I have the pleasure of hosting the introductory session on SAP Business Data Cloud. Think of it as your 20-minute guided tour of the product: what it is, how it's positioned, and where it fits into SAP's overall data and analytics strategy.

I'm running this session twice:

  • 📅 Monday, May 11 🕜 1:30 PM - 1:50 PM EDT
  • 📅 Tuesday, May 12 🕓 4:00 PM - 4:20 PM EDT

🔗  Link to the session: https://www.sap.com/events/sapphire/orlando/flow/sap/so26/catalog/page/catalog/session/1772570877449001qvJ7

The session is perfect for anyone who hasn't had a close look at SAP BDC yet. You'll get a clear overview of the full offering — from SAP Datasphere for data modeling and preparation, to SAP Analytics Cloud for analytics, planning, and consumption, all the way to Joule as the AI-powered interface layer. I'll also point you toward the deeper sessions covering topics like AI, analytics, and partner integrations — because with 30+ sessions on Business Data Cloud at this year's SAPPHIRE, there's plenty more to explore.

Come find me in Orlando

Beyond the sessions, I'll also be at the SAP Business Data Cloud booth — we have six demo stations and a great team ready to answer your questions. There are also hands-on sessions if you want to get your fingers on the keyboard. So, no excuse when you are in Orlando. Let's meet and have a chat.

Can't make it to Orlando? No problem:

➡️  I publish a monthly newsletter 📰 on the SAP Community covering all things BDC and Datasphere — check it out and follow me https://profile.sap.com/u/kpsauer or also on LinkedIn https://www.linkedin.com/in/klaus-peter-sauer/

➡️  Great content on YouTube 🎥, including the "New Era of Business Data" series covering all areas of SAP BDC including licensing
🔗  https://events.sap.com/data-analytics/en_us/home.html and my monthly top features video playlist
🔗  https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL3ZRUb1AKkpS8bwmAREXo78O2xccXzoiy

➡️  There's a free trial 🆓 of SAP Datasphere available — no excuses not to explore it yourself
🔗  https://www.sap.com/products/data-cloud/trial.html

One question for you 🫵

SAP Business Data Cloud is now roughly one year old. I'm curious: what capability or feature do you feel is still missing — and what value would it bring you? Drop your thoughts in the comments 👇.
I'd love to hear what the community is thinking, and it'll help shape where we focus next.

See you in Orlando! 🌴

entry.title: Re: How Asset wise depreciation details can be seen from...
entry.content:
Hello!

The depreciation run is designed primarily to post accounting entries, not to produce detailed reports. The system creates accounting documents summarized at ledger / G/L account level.

  • Each document includes multiple assets
  • When you open a document, you can see several assets grouped together in the line items

The “Schedule Asset Accounting Jobs” app:

  • Displays posting documents
  • Does not provide a dedicated asset-wise list view

Although asset information exists within the document line items, there is no direct asset-by-asset report in this app output.

For asset-level visibility, SAP provides standard reporting apps:

  1. Asset Transactions  
  2. Asset Balances

entry.title: Re: How to change the rescheduling date in MD04 by using...
entry.content: In Public Cloud, it is not feasible to change MD04 rescheduling dates. Possible workaround is to build a custom analytical report that compares rescheduling proposals against the planned calendar and highlights deviations.

entry.title: Product-Agent Fit: The New Standard for Building on SAP BTP in the Age of Joule
entry.content:
A framework for SAP architects, developers, and product managers navigating the agentic enterprise

The Question SAP Is Already Asking

At SAP TechEd 2025 in Berlin, SAP Executive Board Member Muhammad Alam, in charge of SAP Product & Engineering, said SAP was entering “the era of agentic AI, where AI moves from being just a tool to becoming your trusted teammate."

A few months later, on March 31, 2026, SAP CEO Christian Klein went further in his blog: "Software is becoming the operating system for trusted autonomy." (Klein, March: “Software is Entering Its Most Powerful Era”)

These are not marketing statements. They are architectural declarations — and they have direct implications for every SAP developer, consultant, and product manager building on BTP today.

Here is the question they force us to ask about every application we build or extend:

Not "will a user love this?" — but "will Joule reach for it?"

That shift — from human satisfaction to agent invocability — is what I want to explore in this post through a framework I have been developing: Product-Agent Fit (PAF).

 

What Is Product-Agent Fit?

For nearly two decades, the dominant lens for evaluating software products has been Product-Market Fit (PMF) — the degree to which a product satisfies a human market's needs well enough that users adopt it, pay for it, and recommend it. PMF assumes the validator of product value is always a human being with preferences, emotions, and tolerance for rough edges.

Product-Agent Fit (PAF) proposes a different validator: the AI agent.

PAF is the degree to which a product's capability surface is naturally consumable by AI agents — such that agents discover it autonomously, invoke it correctly, trust its outputs, chain it into workflows, and operate within enterprise governance constraints — without human mediation at invocation time.

In SAP terms: does your CAP application, your Fiori app, your S/4HANA extension — does it work when Joule calls it? Not when a user clicks through it. When an agent invokes it.

The answer to that question is increasingly the most important thing you would know about the long-term relevance of whatever you are building.

 

Why This Matters Specifically on SAP BTP

SAP has been executing a clear, multi-year trajectory toward agent-first architecture:

  • September 2023: Joule launched as a copilot — human-facing, UI-adjacent
  • November 2024: Joule went agentic — multi-agent orchestration, coordinating "across business functions" (TechEd 2024)
  • April 2025: SAP joined Google as a founding industry contributor to the A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocol — standardizing how agents communicate across enterprise platforms
  • November 2025: SAP unveiled major advancements in agentic AI by introducing new Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers at TechEd 2025, enabling Claude Code, Cursor, Cline, and Windsurf to access SAP development frameworks directly
  • March 2026: Christian Klein: "Building an agent is becoming increasingly easier—the tip of the iceberg. Deploying it across end-to-end supply chains or financial close processes, with full compliance and audit trails, is where most of the effort lies."

The direction is unambiguous. SAP is rebuilding its platform so that Joule — and agents beyond Joule — can consume every capability without a human navigating a screen.

The question for us as builders is: are the things we build on BTP keeping pace with that direction?

 

The PAF Framework — Mapped to SAP

I propose eleven dimensions for evaluating whether an SAP BTP application has achieved Product-Agent Fit. Each maps to specific SAP tools and patterns you can act on today.

Two SAP resources serve as the primary references throughout this framework. For agent-layer implementation — Joule Studio, MCP tooling, agent architecture, and the AI development lifecycle — SAP's AI Golden Path (architecture.learning.sap.com/docs/aigp) is the dedicated guide. For platform security, CAP patterns, ORD, and audit logging — the SAP BTP Developer's Guide applies. Where a dimension touches both layers, both are called out.

1. Capability Fit

Are your capabilities atomic enough for an agent to invoke directly?

In SAP terms: your OData V4 services, your CAP action handlers, your ABAP logic exposed as OData V4 services via RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) — are they scoped at the level of a single, invocable agent action? Not "manage the purchase order" but release_purchase_order(po_id, approver_id).

The 2,100+ pre-delivered Joule skills SAP has published by the end of 2025 are each scoped exactly this way. If you are building custom extensions, your capability granularity should match that standard.

SAP tool: CAP action definitions, OData V4 services built on RAP

2. Interface Fit

Is your API surface natively machine-readable without human explanation?

This is where MCP comes in. At TechEd 2025, SAP announced that developers using agentic coding tools can now access SAP development frameworks via SAP Build local MCP Servers. The open-source SAP MDK MCP Server (github.com/SAP/mdk-mcp-server) exposes Mobile Development Kit tools as MCP tools consumable by any MCP-compatible agent.

For your custom BTP application, Interface Fit means: is there a machine-readable, typed schema that describes what your service does, what parameters it takes, and what errors it returns — in a form an LLM can reason over without a human explaining it?

SAP tools: MCP Server definitions, OData V4 with typed EDM schemas, SAP AI SDK (Javascript: github.com/SAP/ai-sdk-js; Java: github.com/SAP/ai-sdk-java)

3. Reliability Fit

Are your outputs deterministic enough for agents to chain without verification overhead?

Christian Klein stated this precisely in his March 2026 blog post: "orchestration, policy enforcement, and workflow determinism are the gatekeepers of trust."

In practice, this means your business logic — especially anything involving financial calculations, inventory, or compliance — must be deterministic. Joule should call your service and trust the result. It should not need to verify the output or ask a human to confirm before passing it to the next step.

This is the separation-of-concerns principle that CAP embodies, now applied to agent consumption: your service layer handles determinism; Joule handles the reasoning. Do not mix these responsibilities.

SAP tool: CAP handlers with deterministic business logic, CDS-defined validation rules

4. Context Fit

Can Joule determine when and how to invoke your service from its schema alone?

SAP's Knowledge Graph (announced TechEd 2024) maps "relationships and context across SAP's vast data landscape" using fifty years of business process expertise — so Joule knows the semantics of your business domain, not just the syntax of your API.

For your custom application, this means: tool descriptions must be semantically rich enough for an LLM to correctly select your tool from a catalog of hundreds. A tool named processMaterial with no description will not be selected. A tool named check_material_availability with the description "Returns current stock level and ATP date for a given material in a specified plant" will be.

SAP tools: MCP tool descriptions, Joule Studio skill definitions, CDS annotations

5. Composability Fit

Does your service output feed directly into the next Joule step without transformation?

SAP's industry partnership in the A2A protocol (April 2025) is a direct investment in this dimension. A2A enables Joule to coordinate with agents from Google, Microsoft, and other vendors — "sharing context and acting together across enterprise platforms without requiring manual system switching or data loss."

For your application: every service response should be structured JSON that the next tool in the chain can consume directly. If your response is a formatted HTML string or a PDF attachment, Joule cannot chain it — a human must intervene. That is a composability failure.

SAP tools: Structured CDS projections, A2A-compliant agent registration, Joule Studio orchestration

6. Headless Deployment Fit

Does your application deliver full value without a human opening a browser?

SAP repositioned Joule from AI copilot to "AI orchestrator across your entire organization" (February 2025) — meaning Joule should be able to execute complete workflows end-to-end, not just assist a human who is already on a screen.

Ask this about your application: if I remove the Fiori UI entirely, can Joule still accomplish the core business task? If the answer is no — because some step requires a human to click Confirm in the UI — that is a Headless Deployment Fit gap.

The fix is not to remove the UI. It is to ensure that human confirmation steps are exposed as structured approval actions that agents can surface through conversation, not as UI buttons that block the workflow.

SAP tools: CAP action handlers callable by MCP, Joule-invocable approval flows on SAP Build Work Zone

7. Trust Pipeline Fit

Does your application enforce governance at the API layer, not just the UI layer?

Walter Sun, SVP and Global Head of AI at SAP, has been direct: "accountability must always rest with humans or legal entities, never with the AI itself" (Sun, June 2025: “From Assistive AI to Agentic AI: Risks, Responsibilities, and the Road Ahead”). And Christian Klein identifies "validation rules, compliance checks, approval flows, identity management and audit trails" as non-negotiable requirements for enterprise agents (Klein, March 2026).

In practice, this means authorization enforcement must happen at your CAP service layer — not just at the Fiori UI. For new applications, this applies whether you are using XSUAA or the newer SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) and Authorization Management Service (AMS) stack that the SAP BTP Developer's Guide now recommends; the governance requirement is identical under both. An agent that bypasses the UI must encounter the same authorization gates as a human user. Every agent-invoked write should be logged to the SAP Audit Log Service with full provenance.

If your authorization logic lives only in the Fiori app and your CAP services are essentially open to any authenticated caller, you have a Trust Pipeline Fit gap — and a serious security risk in an agentic world.

SAP tools: Authorization enforcement in CAP handlers, SAP Audit Log Service, SAP Authorization and Trust Management

8. Longitudinal Context Fit

Does your application give Joule access to accumulated business knowledge, not just current-state data?

SAP's Business Data Cloud creates "a single trusted data layer" across SAP and non-SAP sources. The SAP Knowledge Graph leverages "50-plus years of business process expertise" to give agents semantic understanding of business relationships that raw data cannot provide.

For your custom application: does Joule know that this supplier has had three late deliveries in the past six months? Does it know that this customer's contract expires in 30 days? Does it understand the regulatory history of this tax position? If that context is locked in a data warehouse that Joule cannot query, your application cannot achieve longitudinal context fit.

SAP tools: SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine (for RAG), SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine, SAP Analytics Cloud, CDS-defined analytical views

9. Surface-Agnostic Rendering Fit

Does your application reach the human wherever they are, not only inside Fiori?

Joule has been extended beyond SAP applications into third-party applications via WalkMe integration (SAP Sapphire, 2025), and Slack adoption for SAP agent interactions has grown significantly. The underlying principle is the same one Salesforce articulates for headless commerce and CRM — that the engagement surface can change (mobile app, Slack, Teams, voice interface) while the underlying business platform stays consistent: "The surface changes. The platform doesn't."

For your application: can the agent surface your workflow outcomes as a Slack notification, a Teams card, a mobile push alert — without you having to build a separate integration for each? If your application only surfaces results in Fiori, you are missing the engagement surfaces where your users actually spend their time.

SAP tools: SAP Build Work Zone, Joule in Slack integration, SAP Mobile Services, SAP BTP notification services

10. Agent Operational Maturity Fit

Can you detect and fix Joule behavioral drift without waiting for a user complaint?

SAP ships the LeanIX AI Agent Hub — dashboards showing agent deployment, process impact, and performance metrics. This is the SAP-side equivalent of what Salesforce calls its Testing Center and Observability tooling.

The critical insight from the industry: agents are probabilistic, not deterministic. There is no bug to fix — only behavior to observe and tune. If your Joule skill works correctly today but drifts after the next model update or business rule change, you need operational tooling to detect that drift before it affects production processes.

SAP tools: LeanIX AI Agent Hub, SAP AI Core evaluation service, Joule Studio agent testing

11. Marketplace Discovery Fit

Is your application discoverable by agents, not just by humans browsing the SAP Marketplace?

ORD — Open Resource Discovery is SAP's answer to this dimension. Now governed by the Linux Foundation Europe and NeoNephos Foundation (open-resource-discovery.org), ORD enables applications to "self-describe their exposed resources and capabilities" in a standardized, decentralized format. When your BTP application publishes ORD metadata, Joule can discover and compose your capabilities without a developer manually wiring the integration.

If your application is not publishing ORD metadata, it is invisible to the next generation of agent-based orchestration — regardless of how well it works when a human finds it manually.

SAP tools: ORD specification implementation in CAP, SAP Business Accelerator Hub publication, Joule Studio as a unified catalog and registration system 

 

The SAP PAF Maturity Ladder

Where does your application sit today?

Level 0 — INVISIBLE

  UI-only. No OData/CAP API. Joule cannot reach it.

  Example: Legacy Excel-based processes, classic Dynpro without API layer.

 

Level 1 — REACHABLE

  OData API exists but no ORD metadata, no MCP tools defined.

  Joule can be manually wired but won't discover it autonomously.

  Example: Standard CAP app without ORD publication.

 

Level 2 — CALLABLE

  OData V4 with typed schemas.

 Joule can invoke with explicit developer instruction. Errors are structured.

  Example: CAP app following the SAP BTP Developer's Guide, ORD partially configured.

 

Level 3 — DISCOVERABLE

  ORD metadata published. MCP tools defined per the SAP AI Golden Path.

  Joule Studio or other agent tooling can discover and wire the capability with much less manual effort.

  Example: Full SAP BTP Developer's Guide + SAP AI Golden Path + MCP + ORD.

 

Level 4 — PREFERRED

  Joule selects autonomously. Authorization enforced at CAP layer.

  Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) approval flows agent-accessible.

  Longitudinal context available via Business Data Cloud.

  Registered as A2A-compatible via SAP Agent Fabric / BTP agent runtime.

  Example: Production Joule Agent with full BTP governance.

 

Level 5 — FOUNDATIONAL

  Other Joule agents and third-party agents depend on yours as a building block.

  Discoverable within Joule Studio without manual wiring.

  Example: What SAP's own HANA Cloud and Master Data services are positioned to become.

 

What to Do This Sprint

With your application's level on the PAF Maturity Ladder identified, here is a concrete sprint-level action plan for each transition.

Moving from Level 1 to Level 2: Publish your CAP service with a typed OData V4 schema. Define structured error responses using CAP's error handling framework. Ensure every action has a meaningful, LLM-readable description in your CDS annotations.

Moving from Level 2 to Level 3: Implement ORD metadata in your CAP application. Define at least one MCP tool wrapping your most-used CAP action. SAP's AI Golden Path (architecture.learning.sap.com/docs/aigp) is the recommended starting point for MCP tool definitions, Joule Studio registration, and the agent development lifecycle. Test that Joule can invoke your tool without a human explaining the parameters.

Moving from Level 3 to Level 4: Move authorization enforcement into your CAP service handlers — not just Fiori app routing. Add Audit Log Service integration for every agent-invoked write. Design your human approval flows as CAP actions that return structured confirmation tokens, not as UI buttons. Register your agent as A2A-compatible via SAP Agent Fabric or the BTP agent runtime to enable cross-vendor agent interoperability.

Moving from Level 4 to Level 5: Document your MCP tool definitions in a format LLMs can parse. Publish relevant APIs, packages, or reusable assets where appropriate. Consider submitting your Joule skill to the SAP partner ecosystem so other agent stacks can discover and reuse your capability.

 

The Framing That Changes How You Build

I want to close with Christian Klein's formulation, because I think it is the most important framing for every SAP practitioner today:

"Building an agent is becoming increasingly easier—the tip of the iceberg. Deploying it across end-to-end supply chains or financial close processes, with full compliance and audit trails, is where most of the effort lies."

That sentence captures the challenge precisely. The hard part is not building the agent. The hard part is making your platform ready for the agent — so that when Joule arrives at your CAP service, your OData endpoint, your approval workflow, it finds something it can invoke, trust, govern, and chain.

That readiness is Product-Agent Fit.

And based on the trajectory of SAP's own platform — A2A, MCP, ORD, Joule Studio, LeanIX AI Agent Hub — it is clear that SAP is building PAF into the platform layer. The question is whether the applications we build on top of that platform are keeping pace.

Over to You

I am curious where the SAP community currently stands on this:

  • Are you already designing your BTP applications with Joule invocability in mind, or is agent consumption still a future concern?
  • Has your team published ORD metadata or defined MCP tools yet?
  • Where do most of your current applications sit on the PAF Maturity Ladder?

Drop your thoughts in the comments. The more specific the better — I would love to hear concrete patterns, blockers, and solutions from those already building at the frontier.

entry.title: AI: Steps to Setup Python Programming
entry.content:
Introduction:
 
Python is a general purpose and easy to read high level programming language. Python is used in many areas, including web development, automation, artificial intelligence, and, most importantly, data science it is also skillful at cleaning, transforming, and analyzing data. Python is the frequently used syntax and libraries make it an easy-to-use programming language that is typically used in AI modeling by a programming community when a person gets into the programming world.
 
Installation of Python Environment.
Visual Studio Code Code Editor : its a code editor to write and execute Python code in the most efficient way, you will require one. VS Code is a lightweight, free and popular Python-friendly editor.
 
  1. Install VS code on its official site and update it to the latest version. http://code.visualstudio.com/download
  2. Press Win + R, then type in %AppData, press Enter. This opens a directory in which Python related settings can be.
  3. Click on System Properties Environment Variables Path. Add the following route . Ex C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python312\Scripts\ , This makes Python commands, such as python and pip, work in command prompt.
  4. Develop a specific folder on your laptop to which you will save all Python files. In VS Code, open this folder by File Open Folder.
 
Following VS Code extensions are recommended.
  1. GitHub Copilot and GitHub Copilot Chat. : Assists you in writing code at a higher rate through AI suggestions.

     

  2. Python & Python Debugger Extension: It gives syntax, debugging features and code execution.

  3.  

  4. Rainbow CSV Extension: Eases the life of CSV (data) files, color-coding columns.
  6.  
  7. VSCode PDF Viewer: This will enable you to open PDF files and view them within VS Code.

 

Git installation
Go to git-scm/download/win and downlod ( Mac)

 

Check git version 

Go to Github.com and signup

Go to account->settings-Developer settings ->generate Pat token

 

Token generated

Create a new repository

 

 

Step to clone the Git repository on local desktop

Execute following common in your terminal  "git clone https://github.com/{GitUsername}/{repository name}"


Step to Start development in Vscode:

Visual Studio -> Openfolder-> repository folder on desktop 

Go to repository -> create folder "Python Programs" -> Create your first Python file with extension .py and execute

 

Enjoy!

 

Summary
 
Python is a dynamic and easy to use programming language which is suited to both beginners and experts.  It needs to be configured by above the steps.

entry.title: ABAP Cloud Logger
entry.content:
Introduction

Logging in ABAP is one of those things that sounds boring until you've done it five times and realized you've written five different solutions. How many custom loggers have you written in your ABAP career? I've lost count.

The scenario keeps repeating. One developer needs structured messages, another insists on SLG1, and the business wants a JSON dump for a dashboard. Three projects later, and you’re deep in the FMs BAL_LOG_CREATE and BAL_LOG_MSG_ADD, maintaining a massive Z-class that is somehow totally different from the one you wrote for the last project.

Τhen came ABAP Cloud. Τhose old function modules weren't released anymore. SAP swapped out the BAL_* family for the cl_bali_* classes (like cl_bali_log and cl_bali_header_setter). The new API is better. Cleaner, properly object-oriented, makes more sense once you get it. The problem is getting it — there's a learning curve, and almost no community tooling around it yet.  Actually, the only one that I have found is this. Shoutout to the author( Björn Schulz )for the inspiration.

So, I decided to build a new one from scratch. With a few rules:

  • ABAP Cloud compatible. No legacy types, no unreleased APIs. Has to run on Steampunk and Embedded Steampunk natively.

  • One entry point for everything. Free text, symsg, exceptions, BAPIRET2 tables, or an arbitrary structure I want dumped to JSON — I shouldn't have to think about which method to call.

  • A fluent API. The kind where the code reads almost like a sentence.

  • Multiton instances keyed on object / subobject / external number. Two unrelated parts of the same transaction should be able to log to the same place without passing instances around, and without paying the cost of recreating loggers every time.

  • All those small things I keep hardcoding anyway. Built in, once.

    What came out of all this is ZCL_CLOUD_LOGGER, a fully open-source logger built specifically for ABAP Cloud.

    In this post, I’ll show you exactly how it works, dive into some of the architectural choices (and bugs I hit along the way), and explain how you can easily drop it into your own systems.

    Installation

    If you've used abapGit before, you already know the drill. If not, this is the moment to install abapGit. Ιt's the standard tool for ABAP open source distribution.

    The github project of cloud logger can be found here.

    The simplest possible example

    Once installed, here's what the absolute minimum looks like:

    DATA(log) = zcl_cloud_logger=>get_instance(
  object    = 'Z_MY_LOG_OBJ'
  subobject = 'STEP_1' ).

log->log_string_add( 'Process started.' ).

" ... do stuff ...

log->log_string_add( 'Process finished.' )
   ->save_application_log( ).

    Logging anything

    The standard SAP API forces you to juggle completely different calls depending on whether you're dealing with a BAPIRET2 table, an exception class, or just a standard sy-msg. My class still gives you specific methods for those, but behind the scenes, everything is routed through the exact same core logic. This guarantees your log output stays consistent, no matter what kind of data you just tossed at it. Υou can also log arbitrary data like a serialized json.

    " A symsg-style message
log->log_message_add( symsg = VALUE #( msgty = 'E'
                                        msgid = 'ZMY_MSGS'
                                        msgno = '042'
                                        msgv1 = 'Order 100' ) ).

" An exception
TRY.
    do_something_risky( ).
  CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(err).
    log->log_exception_add( exception = err
                            severity  = 'E' ).
ENDTRY.

" Whole BAPIRET2 table — and only the errors
log->log_bapiret2_table_add( bapiret2_t   = lt_bapi_messages
                             min_severity = 'E' ).

" Log serialized to JSON automatically
log->log_data_add( data = ls_my_payload ).

    Sticky context

    Say you're looping through 50 sales orders, and each one goes through multiple validations. If one fails, your log needs to show exactly which order caused it. Without sticky context, you're forced to manually concatenate the order ID into every single string before logging it. With sticky context, you just set it once per iteration:

    log->set_context( |Order { order_id }| ).

log->log_string_add( 'validation started' ).
log->log_string_add( 'price check OK' ).
log->log_string_add( 'stock check failed' ).

log->clear_context( ).

    Built-in timing

    Every project eventually gets a requirement like "how long did this specific step take?". Since I always end up writing the same timer logic anyway, I just so I built in it directly into the logger:

    log->start_timer( ).

call_external_service( ).

log->stop_timer( 'External service call' ).

    The internal error trail

    I'll admit, almost every logger I've ever written has the same dirty secret somewhere in the code: CATCH cx_bali_runtime INTO DATA(swallowed). " ignore.You obviously don't want a simple read method like log_contains_error to trigger a dump and blow up the calling program. So, you just swallow the exception. Works fine until something actually breaks and you have no trace of what happened.

    DATA(errors) = log->get_internal_errors( ).
" Returns a table of timestamp / method_name / error_text
" for every cx_bali_runtime that was caught silently.

    Multiton config validation

    There is a specific flaw with the multiton pattern.If two callers ask for the exact same (object, subobject, ext_number) key but pass different db_save flags, the second caller just silently gets the first caller's instance. Then they waste time debugging why their db_save = abap_false log ended up being saved to the database anyway. To prevent this,  the logger checks the config and throws if there's a mismatch.

    " First caller
zcl_cloud_logger=>get_instance(
  object = 'Z_LOG' subobject = 'STEP' db_save = abap_true ).

" Second caller, later in the same transaction
zcl_cloud_logger=>get_instance(
  object = 'Z_LOG' subobject = 'STEP' db_save = abap_false ).
" → raises zcx_cloud_logger_error: config_mismatch

    Full Example

    Here is a full example based on a real world project:

    DATA(log) = zcl_cloud_logger=>get_instance(
  object               = 'Z_INVOICE'
  subobject            = 'POSTING'
  enable_emergency_log = abap_true ).

log->set_context( |Invoice { invoice_id }| )
   ->start_timer( ).

TRY.
    post_invoice( invoice_id ).
    log->log_string_add( string = 'Posted successfully'
                         msgty  = log->c_message_type-success ).

  CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(err).
    log->log_exception_add( exception = err severity = 'E' ).
ENDTRY.

log->stop_timer( 'Invoice posting' )
   ->clear_context( )
   ->save_application_log( ).

    Conclusion

    Logging is the kind of code nobody notices when it works. The whole point of this class was to stop solving the same problem on every project and have something I can drop in and forget about. Fluent API, multiton, sticky context, timing, JSON dumps for the weird requirements — all the stuff I kept rebuilding by hand.

    The project is open source, MIT-licensed and runs in ABAP Cloud. I've worked through some bugs to get it stable, and it's now in real use. If you give it a try, tell me what's missing. Feel free to open an issue,pull request or just send me a message.Find it on GitHub.

entry.title: Understanding Single Sign On (SSO) in SAP BTP with IAS and Microsoft Entra ID
entry.content:
Hi,

In this blog, I will explain how Single Sign-On works in SAP BTP environment using SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS) integrated with an external identity provider like Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD).

This blog is the first part of a two-part series. Part 1 focuses on concepts and architecture.

While Part 2 will focus on Technical setup and configuration.

So, what is SSO?

Single Sign-On is like a master key. You use it to unlock the main door, and from there, every other door you are permitted to enter opens automatically — no extra keys needed.

Imagine your organization has 10 applications, and you have access to 5 of them — each with its own separate set of credentials. Keeping track of all those usernames and passwords can quickly become frustrating and overwhelming.

With SSO, you log in just once, and you are automatically granted access to every application you are permitted to use — no additional logins required.

 

In this article, we explore an enterprise identity architecture that leverages Microsoft Entra ID in conjunction with SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS) to establish a robust and scalable authentication mechanism — enabling users to securely access SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and a wide range of SAP applications.

As illustrated in the diagram below, the architecture is structured around three distinct layers:

Microsoft Entra ID assumes the role of the Leading Identity Provider (IdP), serving as the authoritative system responsible for verifying user identities, enforcing Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), and applying conditional access policies.

SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS) operates as the Proxy Identity Provider, acting as a trusted intermediary that brokers authentication requests between Microsoft Entra ID and the downstream SAP applications.

Finally, the SAP applications themselves — including SAP BTP (subaccount), SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Success Factors, and others — function as the Service Providers (SP), relying on the assertions issued through this identity chain to grant users seamless and secure access.

When a user initiates a login request, it is routed through this layered identity framework. Microsoft Entra ID evaluates the request against the defined authentication and access policies, and issues either an approval or a denial accordingly. Upon successful authentication, a SAML assertion is generated and passed back through SAP IAS to the target SAP application, granting the user access. This structured request-and-response mechanism forms the foundation of how user identities are validated and how access to enterprise applications is governed within a Single Sign-On environment — ensuring a secure, consistent, and seamless experience across the entire SAP landscape. 

       Key Benefits of bringing in IAS as a proxy IdP

  1. Isolation and Buffering - isolate SAP systems from direct exposure to external authentication flows
  2. Audit and Monitoring - detailed logs of authentication attempts, failures, and user activity
  3. Failover and Redundancy – IAS supports multiple IdPs, in case Azure Entra is not available or the organization wants to bring in another external IdP.

 

Approaches to Single Sign-On

There are two approaches to establishing SSO

Option 1: Service Provider initiated SSO

Assuming in this example, SAP BTP sub account is the service provider and that is where the user wants to login. We call it ‘SAP app’.

The user will directly hit the SAP app URL

SAP app redirects to IAS instead of asking the password itself (Request 1)

SAP IAS doesn’t authenticate; it delegates because IAS recognizes that Entra ID is the master IdP (Request 2)

Entra ID authenticates the user, this is where the actual login happens, credentials are asked – userID, password, MFA (if enabled)

Once authenticated, Entra ID sends the assertion back to IAS (SAML Response/Response 2)

IAS passes the assertion to the SAP app (Response 1)

User gets access: The SAP app trusts IAS, IAS trusted MS Entra ID, Entra ID verified the user and so the user is allowed to access the SAP app.

Note that in SP initiated SSO, SP creates an authentication request which Includes:

  • Request ID
  • Timestamp
  • Audience (SP entity ID)
  • RelayState / redirect target

And the IdP responds to it.

Used responses are rejected, hence this method is more secure and auditable, Strong binding between request and response.

 

Option 2: Identity Provider initiated SSO

 
Have a look at the diagram, the user logs in directly to the IdP – Microsoft Entra ID

 

Microsoft Entra asks for the credentials, userID, password and MFA (if enabled), user authentication is done here

 

 

Entra ID sends SAML assertion to SAP IAS, essentially saying "this user just logged in, here's everything you need to know about them”

IAS accepts the assertion, looks up the user in its directory, and applies any of its own rules

The user now navigates the launchpad — SuccessFactors, Ariba, S/4HANA, etc. They click one.

Since the user is already authenticated, IAS immediately forwards the trust to the chosen SAP app. No redirect back to MS Entra ID. No login prompt again.

User gets access to SAP app.

 

It is important to note that in this scenario there is no request from the application, only the response is sent from IdP.

There is no original request to validate against and hence nothing to mark the response as already used. This opens the door to:

  • Replay attacks
  • Assertion reuse
  • Session confusion

 

In the second part, I will walk through the SSO setup and configuration steps, covering trust establishment, role of IAS, IAS groups and integration considerations between SAP BTP and the enterprise IdP.

 

Regards, 

Shoeb

entry.title: Requirement for a solution to Mass Update Asset Master Data
entry.content:
We need a solution that will allow to edit the "UL in Years" in "Valuation" Tab in the "Manage Fixed Assets" app at mass scale, since there're more than 2000 assets having a wrong entry in these fields, we need a solution where we can point out this particular field and edit its value against the asset number.

In the Valuation Section, the UL in Years is mistakenly uploaded as 4 under the Depreciation Area 15, the same is happened with around 2000 Assets, which needs to be edited at mass scale, editing manually each and every asset is time consuming so we're looking for an automated solution where this particular fields data can be edited.

entry.title: May 2026 Developer Challenge - From events to intelligence: Building an event-driven RAG pipeline
entry.content:
This month's developer challenge is a bit different from previous ones. Instead of focusing exclusively on SAP technologies, the goal is for you to learn the concepts, architect a solution and implement it using whatever tools you have access to — SAP, open-source, trial, paid products, use your favourite programming language or whatever agent you fancy using. At the end of each week's challenge, I will share how I solved it, but the path you take to get there is yours to choose.

Here is what we will build over the four weeks: An event-driven integration pipeline that consumes events from a broker, vectorizes the data within those event payloads, stores the vectors in a vector store, and makes them available for Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG).

The screenshot below can give you an idea of what a RAG pipeline can look like. If you want to learn more about how the integration flow below was built - check this out.

This is a challenge that sits at the intersection of two worlds that are increasingly converging: event-driven architectures and Generative AI. Understanding how to feed real-time, event-driven data into an AI pipeline is becoming a foundational skill — and that's exactly what we're going to build.

Links to each week will be added here as they are published:

  • Week 1: Getting familiar with the Business Events (To be published  4th of May)
  • Week 2: Connecting to the broker and consuming Business Events
  • Week 3: Vectorizing the event payload
  • Week 4: Storing vectors and enabling RAG
